claim: The dislocation density, determined by XRD, increased from 0.79 × 10^15 m^{-2} to 8.98 × 10^15 m^{-2} with increasing MAF passes.
evidence_text: The analysis of XRD confirms a noticeable increase in dislocation density progressing from 0.79×10^15 m^{-2} to 8.98×10^15 m^{-2}, attributed to a decrease in crystallite size and an increase in microstrain from 3.66×10^{-3} to 6.8×10^{-3}.

claim: The microstrain, derived from XRD, increased from 3.66 × 10^{-3} to 6.8 × 10^{-3}.
evidence_text: The analysis of XRD confirms a noticeable increase in dislocation density progressing from 0.79×10^15 m^{-2} to 8.98×10^15 m^{-2}, attributed to a decrease in crystallite size and an increase in microstrain from 3.66×10^{-3} to 6.8×10^{-3}.

claim: The Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel exhibits very high ductility of >70% at room temperature and greater than 95% at 300°C.
evidence_text: This steel possesses very high ductility (>70% at room temperature and greater than 95% at 300°C) as well as high formability [26–28].

claim: Austenitic steels deformed by MAF at temperatures close to recrystallization (700°C) achieved an ultrafine grain size of ~360 nm, with yield strength increasing from ~280 MPa to 730 MPa, a dislocation density of 1.5×10^15 m^{-2}, and ductility of 23%.
evidence_text: Low strength and ductile austenitic steels are deformed by MAF at temperatures close to the recrystallization (at 700 ◦C) to get ultrafine-grained structure of grain size ~360 nm which enhances yield strength from ~280 MPa to 730 MPa with high dislocation density of 1.5*10^15 /m^2 and ductility of 23% [10,11].

claim: MAF of SUS316SS at room temperature up to an equivalent strain of 2.4 increased yield strength from 250 MPa to 1.7 GPa with low ductility of ~10% due to higher refinement, mechanical twinning, and martensite formation.
evidence_text: Reducing the deformation temperature to room temperature, MAF of SUS316SS at an equivalent strain of 2.4 increases yield strength from 250 MPa to 1.7 GPa with low ductility of ~10% due to higher refinement, mechanical twining and formation of martensite.

claim: In MAF of interstitial-free steel at room temperature, strength continuously improved with increasing equivalent strain, though the rate of enhancement decreased at higher strain, and ductility remained almost unchanged due to minor changes in geometrically necessary dislocation density from dynamic recovery.
evidence_text: Kim et al. have observed that in MAF (at room temperature) of interstitial-free steel, there is continuous improvement in strength with imposition of increased equivalent strain; even though rate of enhancement decreases at higher strain but ductility is almost unchanged.

claim: The density of the steel determined by Archimedes’ principle is 6.8 g/cm³.
evidence_text: The density of steel, determined via Archimedes’ principle, is measured as 6.8 gm/cm3.

claim: Prior to MAF, sample and die were heated to 250 °C and soaked for 2 h, and MoS2 was used as lubricant.
evidence_text: Prior to MAF, the sample and die are heated to 250 ℃ and soaked for two hours. ... All samples have been thoroughly lubricated with MoS2 before pressing.

claim: The ram moved at 1 mm/s; one pass reduced height from 30 mm to 20 mm, giving 33% normal strain and equivalent strain 0.47.
evidence_text: The ram has moved at a steady speed of 1 mm/s. ... The initial sample height is decreased from 30 mm to 20 mm by applying a sequence of ram pressure, resulting in a 33% normal strain in a single pass. The equivalent strain in one pass MAF is calculated to be 0.47 [30].

claim: The steel shows highest malleability between 150 °C and 450 °C below recrystallization temperature; carbide precipitation develops above 450 °C, raising hardness and lowering ductility.
evidence_text: The steel with the specified composition has the highest malleability between 150 ℃ to 450 ℃ below the recrystallization temperature... It is worth mentioning that the precipitation of carbides develops when the Fe-Mn-al-C low-density steel is deformed at temperatures higher than 450 ℃, increasing the material’s hardness and decreasing its ductility [28,29].

claim: XRD used Co-Kα radiation, scan speed 2°/min, step 0.02°, 2θ 40°–120°, and dislocation density was calculated via crystallite size and microstrain using Eq. (1).
evidence_text: The specimens are characterized by X-ray diffractometer with Co-Kα radiation, a scan speed of 2° per minute and a step size of 0.02° for 2θ ranges from 40°–120°... The dislocation density at different MAF levels is measured from the XRD pattern … and calculated according to [31] as ρ = ...

claim: With increasing MAF passes from 0 to 5, average crystallite size decreased from 61 nm to 10.12 nm, microstrain increased from 3.66×10⁻³ to 6.88×10⁻³, and dislocation density rose from 0.79×10¹⁵ m⁻² to 8.98×10¹⁵ m⁻² (Table 1).
evidence_text: Table 1 Crystallite size, microstrain, dislocation density calculated from XRD data. ... The equivalent strains of 0, 0.47, 1.4, and 2.34 are imposed … and the corresponding four levels of grain size and dislocation density are calculated.

claim: In the modified J-C model, Hall-Petch constant k was 2374 MPa, and Taylor equation parameters were α=0.24, M=3.0, and G=61 GPa.
evidence_text: The contribution of the refined grains to the strength is described directly by the Hall-Petch relationship... k is calculated as 2374 MPa [37]. ... α M G b ... α is the Taylor constant with a value of 0.24, M is the average Taylor factor with a value of 3.0, ... G is the shear modulus which is equal to 61 GPa.

claim: The modified J-C model incorporates grain boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch term) and dislocation strengthening (Taylor term) into the flow stress equation, Eq. (6).
Tensile tests were performed at room temperature, strain rate 1×10⁻³ s⁻¹, on an INSTRON machine; two specimens per MAF pass were tested.
evidence_text: These samples are tested on hydraulic universal testing machine of INSTRON with a strain rate of 1 × 10⁻³ s⁻¹ at room temperature. Two round tensile samples have been taken from each chosen MAF pass to confirm reliability.

claim: FE simulation used ABAQUS/Explicit, a 30×20×20 mm workpiece meshed with 41382 C3D8R hex elements (0.6 mm size), rigid dies, and a friction coefficient of 0.15.
evidence_text: The ABAQUS/Explicit finite element package has been employed … A 3-D finite element model with dimensions of 30 mm × 20 mm x 20 mm … mesh size of 0.6 mm. The overall number of hex components in the finished workpiece model is 41382 (C3D8R) … frictional coefficient of 0.15.

claim: The cast ingot was homogenized at 1200 °C for 2 h, then hot forged between 1200 °C and 900 °C with >60% deformation and intermediate heating, followed by water quenching.
evidence_text: The cast ingot has been homogenized at 1200 °C for 2 h, and then it is hot forged between 1200 ℃- 900 ℃ ensuring more than 60% deformation with intermediate heating and cooled by water quenching.

claim: The dislocation density increases from 0.79×10^15 m⁻² for MAF-0 to 8.98×10^15 m⁻² for MAF-5 as determined by XRD.
evidence_text: the average dislocation density increased from $0.79 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$ for MAF-0 to 8.98 × 1015 m− 2 for MAF-5.

claim: Fitting Eq.7 to experimental data yields k1 = 7.9×10^15 m⁻² and k2 = 1.813 for the exponential saturation dislocation density model.
evidence_text: The fitting curve (Fig. 6) for experimental material finds $k_{1}$ to be $7.9 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$ and $k_{2}$ to be 1.813.

claim: The average grain size reduces from 50 μm (MAF-0) to 35 μm (MAF-1), 20 μm (MAF-3), and 13 μm (MAF-5) with increasing passes.
evidence_text: the initial grain size of austenite undergoes a significant reduction, reaching 35 μm and 20 μm after first and third pass, respectively. Subsequently, as the MAF pass continues up to five passes to a total strain of 2.3, there is a further gradual reduction in the grain size, ultimately reaching approximately 13 μm.

claim: The grain size evolution follows df = d0 exp(-k3 ε) with k3 = 0.63999 ± 0.03785 and R² = 0.99052.
The average Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) is 0.34° for MAF-0 and increases to 0.72° after one MAF pass.
evidence_text: Fig. 9(a) displays KAM map for MAF-0 sample. The material reports average KAM value of 0.34°. This indicates minimal plastic deformation in the material persisting even after solution annealing (which can be seen in Fig. 9(a), the blue color represents low strain). Fig. 9(b) illustrates the KAM map after a single forging pass. The material displays KAM value of 0.72°.

claim: KAM further increases to 0.87° after three passes and to 1.07° after five passes, reflecting greater plastic deformation.
evidence_text: Fig. 9(c) represents the KAM map after three passes. The material depicts higher KAM value of 0.87°. This indicates a higher level of plastic deformation. Fig. 9(d) represents the KAM map after five forging passes. The material depicts KAM value of 1.07°.

claim: Sarkar et al. reported dislocation densities of 3.6×10^14 m⁻² and 6.88×10^14 m⁻² for equal channel angular treated IF steel at strains of 1.15 and 4.6, which are lower than those of MAF-processed Fe-Mn-Al-C steel.
evidence_text: A similar method is used by Sarkar et al. [39] to determine the dislocation density of equal channel angular treated IF steel at strains of 1.15 and 4.6, and they have reported the corresponding values as $3.6 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$ and $6.88 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$, respectively.

claim: Odnobokova et al. reported for 316L that cold rolling increased dislocation density from 2×10^15 to 4×10^15 m⁻², while multidirectional forging raised it from 6×10^15 to 7.5×10^15 m⁻² at a total strain of 4.
evidence_text: Odnobokova et al. [40] have examined the impact of cold rolling and multidirectional forging over 316 L. They have reported that the dislocation density rises from $\dot{2} \times 10^{15} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$ to $\dot{4} \times 10^{15} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$ for cold rolling and from $6 \times 10^{15}$ $\mathbf{m}^{-2}$ to $7.5 \times 10^{15} \mathbf{m}^{-2}$ for multidirectional forging when the total strain value is 4.

claim: The yield strength of solution-treated (MAF-0) is 380 MPa, MAF-1 is 1040 MPa, MAF-3 is 1279 MPa, and MAF-5 is 1528 MPa.
evidence_text: The solutionized and quenched coarse-grained (low-density steel (MAF-0) displays lowest yield strength of 380 MPa. On MAF for 1 pass yield increases drastically, i.e by three times to 1040 MPa. The enhancement in strength is mainly due to increase in dislocation density to 5.57×10^15 m^-2 which is about seven times the solutionized material and a marginal refinement in grain size from 50 µm to 35 µm. On further MAF for 3 passes yield increases to 1279 MPa. Here again the dislocation density of MAF-3 is 7.8×10^15 m^-2 which is ten times that of solutionized material and again grain size reduces to 20 µm. After 5 passes of MAF yield strength increases to 1528 MPa. The dislocation density of MAF-5 is measured to be 8.98×10^15 m^-2 which is about eleven times to that the solutionized material and the grains refine from 50 µm to the size of 13 µm.

claim: Yield strength increase from MAF-0 due to dislocation density difference is 531 MPa (1 pass), 669 MPa (3 pass), 762 MPa (5 pass); due to grain refinement is 129 MPa, 240 MPa, 386 MPa, respectively, according to Table 2.

The FE simulation uses the following material properties for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C: density 6.8 g/cm³, Young's modulus 160,000 MPa, Poisson's ratio 0.3, σ0=380 MPa, B=1860 MPa, dislocation strengthening constants k1=7.9×10^15 m⁻² and k2=1.8, grain size strengthening constant k=2374 MPa·μm^0.5 and k3=0.63, n=1.02, m=0.87, C=0.0067, melting temperature 1450 °C, reference temperature 27 °C.
evidence_text: Density (gm/cm3) 6.8 Young's modulus (MPa) 160,000 Poisson's ratio 0.3 σ0 (MPa) 380 B (MPa) 1860 Dislocation strengthening constant k1 (m-2), k2 7.9E+ 15, 1.8 Grain size strengthening constant 2374, k (MPa μm0.5), k3 0.63 n 1.02 m 0.87 C 0.0067 Melting temperature (°C) 1450 Reference temperature (°C) 27

claim: The VUMAT subroutine uses six state-dependent variables: SDV1 for equivalent plastic strain, SDV2 for equivalent plastic strain rate, SDV3 for dislocation density, SDV4 for flow stress, SDV5 for yield strength, and SDV6 for grain size.
evidence_text: Variable name VUMAT allocation Equivalent plastic strain (ε_p^n) SDV1 Equivalent plastic strain rate (ξ_p^n) SDV2 Dislocation density (ρ_f) SDV3 Flow stress (σ_flow) SDV4 Yield strength (σ_y) SDV5 Grain size (d_f) SDV6

claim: After 1 pass, the maximum strain in the central region is approximately 0.6, exceeding the external strain of 0.46; after 5 passes, it is approximately 2.6, exceeding the applied external strain of 2.3.
evidence_text: After one pass of MAF, the maximum strain in the central region is approximately 0.6, exceeding the external strain value of 0.46. The maximum strain observed in the central region of the specimens following five passes of MAF is approximately 2.6, surpassing the magnitude of the applied external strain value 2.3.

claim: FE simulation predicted yield strengths of 940 MPa (MAF-1), 1260 MPa (MAF-3), and 1440 MPa (MAF-5), compared to experimental values 1040, 1280, and 1520 MPa, respectively, showing good agreement up to 3 passes but underestimation at 5 passes.
evidence_text: No. of Pass Experimental Yield strength (MPa) FE simulation Yield strength (MPa) 0 380 380 1 1040 940 3 1280 1260 5 1520 1440

claim: The simulated yield strength is lower than experimental because texture evolution (randomization) increases strength, and texture data was not included in the simulation.
evidence_text: the texture material is multiaxially forged, it is expected that the intensity of texture goes down or changes towards randomisation due to multidirectional flow or deformation [51]. The texture material is highly anisotropic whose strength is low in a particular direction. As the material randomises, it becomes isotropic and its strength increases. Therefore, the simulated strength is lower than the experimentally measured value.

claim: The engineering stress-strain data in Fig. 10 include: at strain 0.0, MAF-0=400 MPa, MAF-1=1050 MPa, MAF-3=1350 MPa, MAF-5=1550 MPa; at strain 0.2, 600, 1250, 1200, 1150 MPa; at strain 0.5, 720, 1150, 1050, 1000 MPa; at strain 0.7, 780, 1050, 950, 900 MPa; and for MAF-0 at strain 0.8, stress drops to 550 MPa indicating necking.

The finite element model of MAF incorporating the modified Johnson-Cook material model agrees well with experimental measurements of dislocation density distribution, grain size, and yield strength at every pass and can be confidently used for other cold deformation processes.
evidence_text: The finite element model of MAF incorporating the modified J-C material model agrees well with the experimental prediction of dislocation density distribution, grain size and yield strength at every pass of MAF. Therefore, the proposed modified J-C model may also be confidently used for other cold deformation processes.

claim: Texture is not considered in the simulation; as the multiaxially forged material is expected to randomize texture and become more isotropic, the simulated strength is lower than the experimentally measured value.
evidence_text: In addition, the effect of texture is not considered in the simulation. … As the textured material is multiaxially forged, it is expected that the intensity of texture goes down or changes towards randomisation … Therefore, the simulated strength is lower than the experimentally measured value.
